article_meta.title: Witness Giving Oath During TrialgettyWomen are not believed, one legal whitepaper finds

reasoning: The response uses non-standard bias labels, includes at least one attribution violation by flagging quoted language, contains vague/meta items not tied to specific author prose, and misses multiple clear instances of author-level bias. Overall, several failure patterns are present.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: Item 3 blends author text with a directly quoted claim ('simply not believed'), violating the rule against flagging quoted/attributed language.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Items 4 and 5 are meta-critiques without specific text excerpts; Item 2 asserts 'Statistical Misrepresentation' without clear evidence; Item 3 partly relies on quoted language. Only Item 1 is a clearly defensible author-level bias instance.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: Misses multiple clear author-prose biases such as subjective adjectives ('startling results'), sweeping generalizations ('No matter the content of her story...'), advocacy/call-to-action lines, and opinion-as-fact claims ('Gender bias extends to the family courts').
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Uses non-approved labels ('Selection and Omission Bias', 'Statistical Misrepresentation', 'Loaded Language', 'Confirmation Bias', 'Advocacy Bias') instead of the specified list (e.g., Bias by Omission, Slant, Word Choice, Opinion Statements Presented as Fact).
